mechanism: HCG binds the same LH receptors as endogenous LH on testicular Leydig cells, stimulating testosterone production. Unlike LH, which has roughly a 30-minute half-life, HCG's half-life is about 36 hours, giving it sustained gonadal stimulation from infrequent dosing. Low doses maintain intratesticular testosterone during suppression from exogenous testosterone; higher doses are used to restart spermatogenesis and endogenous production after prolonged suppression.
effects: Maintains intratesticular testosterone and spermatogenesis during TRT, helps prevent or reverse testicular atrophy from exogenous testosterone use, and can restore endogenous testosterone production in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism or post-cycle scenarios. Possible side effects include elevated estradiol (via testicular aromatase stimulation), mild injection site reactions, acne, transient testicular discomfort as Leydig cells reactivate, and rare gynecomastia risk if estradiol rises significantly.
results_timeline: Not specified in source with a discrete week-by-week timeline; standard maintenance protocols run 8-16 weeks, while high-dose recovery protocols for restarting spermatogenesis are typically continued 3-6 months.
timing: Subcutaneous injections 3 times weekly (e.g., Mon/Wed/Fri) is sufficient given HCG's long half-life; daily dosing is unnecessary. Consistent injection days/times are recommended, with site rotation across abdomen and thighs.
research_notes: Human Chorionic Gonadotropin: structure, function, and LH receptor binding mechanisms, National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI), https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532950/ | Pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of HCG: extended half-life and sustained activity, Seminars in Reproductive Medicine, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11265525/ | Coviello AD et al., Low-dose hCG maintains intratesticular testosterone in men with testosterone-induced gonadotropin suppression, The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism (2005), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15713727/ | Chorionic Gonadotropin (Pregnyl/Novarel): reconstituted solution stable for 60 days when refrigerated; do not freeze, FDA Prescribing Information, https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/fda/fdaDrugXsl.cfm?setid=f93b2baa-03ac-4650-b35a-20b818d712d6

doses.label: 5000IU
doses.dosage_text: Dosed in IU, not mg. Reconstituting 5000 IU with 2 mL bacteriostatic water yields 2,500 IU/mL, where 1 unit on a U-100 syringe = 25 IU. Standard maintenance is 500 IU (20 units) subcutaneously 3 times weekly (1,500 IU/week total), used to preserve testicular function during TRT. High-dose recovery protocols use 1,500-2,500 IU (60-100 units) 3 times weekly for 3-6 months to restart spermatogenesis after prolonged suppression, then taper to maintenance.

name: HGH 191AA
overview: HGH 191AA is recombinant human growth hormone (somatropin), structurally identical to the growth hormone naturally secreted by the pituitary gland. It's studied for effects on body composition, fat metabolism, and tissue repair, typically dosed once daily by subcutaneous injection to mimic the body's natural GH secretion pattern.
mechanism: As a bioidentical copy of endogenous GH, it acts on GH receptors throughout the body to enhance lipolysis, stimulate protein synthesis, and drive IGF-1 production in the liver. Once-daily subcutaneous dosing, especially at bedtime, is meant to approximate the body's natural nighttime GH secretion pattern rather than deliver a single large pulse.
effects: Clinical literature associates GH administration with increases in lean body mass and reductions in fat mass, enhanced fat oxidation, and (in GH-deficient adults) sustained improvements in muscle strength and body composition with long-term use. Notably, a systematic review in healthy athletes found GH increased lean mass without reliably improving strength or exercise performance. Possible side effects include injection site irritation, fluid retention/edema (more common at higher doses), joint discomfort, and potential effects on glucose metabolism warranting monitoring in extended protocols.
results_timeline: Not specified in source as a discrete short-term timeline; body composition changes were measured over six months in the foundational Rudman study, and long-term GH-deficient adult follow-up studies showed sustained improvements over roughly 10 years of maintenance dosing.
timing: Once daily subcutaneous injection, preferably at bedtime to mirror the body's physiological GH secretion pattern. Injection sites should be rotated systematically to prevent lipoatrophy.
research_notes: Rudman et al., Six months of HGH administration in older men significantly increased lean body mass and decreased adipose mass compared to placebo, N. Engl. J. Med. (1990), https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging/articles/10.3389/fragi.2025.1549453/full | Gotherstrom et al., 10-year adult GH deficiency follow-up showed sustained increases in muscle strength and improved body composition with maintenance dosing, J. Clin. Endocrinol. Metab. (2010), https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging/articles/10.3389/fragi.2025.1549453/full | Liu et al., Systematic review of GH in healthy athletes found GH increased lean body mass but did not significantly improve muscle strength or exercise performance, Ann. Intern. Med. (2008) | Hadzovic et al., Moderate doses of GH (70-400 mcg) in healthy adults produce dose-dependent increases in lipolysis and fat oxidation, Bosn. J. Basic Med. Sci. (2004), https://www.bjbms.org/ojs/index.php/bjbms/article/download/3365/971/12109

doses.label: 10IU (3.33mg)
doses.dosage_text: Conservative replacement-style protocols use 150-500 mcg daily, while advanced metabolic/performance research protocols use 1000-2000 mcg daily. At ~1.11 mg/mL, 200 mcg = 18 units, 500 mcg = 45 units. An 8-week titration schedule starts at 200 mcg daily and increases by roughly 100 mcg per week, injected once daily (subcutaneous, preferably bedtime).

name: HMG
overview: Human Menopausal Gonadotropin (HMG) is a purified gonadotropin preparation providing a 1:1 mix of FSH and LH activity. In male fertility protocols it is used alongside HCG to add the FSH stimulation needed for spermatogenesis, particularly in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism or infertility where HCG alone is insufficient.
mechanism: HMG supplies both FSH and LH activity derived from purified human menopausal urine. FSH is essential for spermatogenesis; when HCG (which mainly drives LH-like testosterone production) fails to induce adequate sperm production on its own, adding HMG's FSH activity supports testicular function and fertility.
effects: Stimulates spermatogenesis in men with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism or secondary infertility, and when combined with HCG has been shown to improve sperm motility, morphology, and concentration, along with increased pregnancy rates in treated couples. Possible side effects include injection site reactions, occasional headache/fatigue/mood changes, gynecomastia from hormonal stimulation, and rare overstimulation or allergic reactions.
results_timeline: Clinical response typically requires a minimum of 12 weeks, with full spermatogenesis taking 3-6 months to develop.
timing: Subcutaneous injection three times weekly (e.g., Monday/Wednesday/Friday), almost always combined with concurrent HCG dosing (2-3 times weekly) for a minimum 12-week course, extendable to 16 weeks.
research_notes: hMG composition: 1:1 blend of FSH and LH derived from postmenopausal urine, Metrovan Urology, https://www.metrovanurology.com/hormones-and-male-infertility | Human menopausal gonadotropin for subcutaneous administration; each vial contains 75 IU FSH + 75 IU LH activity, Menopur (Menotropins) Product Monograph, https://pdf.hres.ca/dpd_pm/00061244.PDF | Randomized trial: HMG 75 IU subcutaneously three times weekly for 3 months significantly improved sperm motility and morphology; pregnancy rate 57% vs. control, International Journal of Reproductive BioMedicine (2014) | Review of hCG and hMG combination therapy for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism; FSH supplementation critical for spermatogenesis when hCG monotherapy inadequate, Reproductive Medicine and Biology (2018), https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6105660/

doses.label: 75IU (0.15mg)
doses.dosage_text: Dosed in IU. Reconstituting the 75 IU vial with 3.0 mL bacteriostatic water yields 25 IU/mL. A full 75 IU dose requires 3.0 mL of solution -- more than a standard 1 mL insulin syringe holds, so it's typically drawn with a 3 mL syringe or split into three 1 mL injections (25 IU each) at different sites in the same session. Standard protocol is 75 IU three times weekly (Mon/Wed/Fri) for a minimum of 12 weeks, usually paired with concurrent hCG therapy.

name: IGF-1 LR3
overview: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 Insulin-like Growth Factor-1) is a modified analog of human IGF-1 engineered for a significantly extended half-life. It's studied in research settings for anabolic and metabolic effects, with once-daily dosing made possible by its reduced binding to IGF binding proteins.
mechanism: IGF-1 LR3 carries an N-terminal 13-amino-acid extension and a glutamic acid substitution at position 3, which sharply reduces its binding affinity to IGF binding proteins. This extends its half-life from the minutes seen with native IGF-1 to several hours, allowing more stable plasma levels and once-daily administration. It exerts anabolic and metabolic effects via IGF-1 receptor binding but has insulin-like activity that requires active blood glucose management.
effects: Associated with enhanced anabolic signaling and protein synthesis, and extended bioavailability versus native IGF-1. The dominant safety concern is hypoglycemia risk from its insulin-like effect on blood glucose (shakiness, confusion, sweating, dizziness), which is why it's typically dosed with food. It has never received regulatory approval for human therapeutic use. Mild injection site irritation is possible; safety data above 50-60 mcg/day is limited, and continuous use beyond 6-8 weeks may lead to diminished response (receptor desensitization).
results_timeline: Not specified in source as a discrete results timeline; dosing follows an 8-week titration/maintenance cycle, with cycling protocols (e.g., 8 weeks on, 4-8 weeks off) used to manage diminishing response over time.
timing: Once daily subcutaneous injection, typically administered in the morning or post-workout, with or shortly after food to mitigate insulin-like effects on blood glucose.
research_notes: Insulin-Like Growth Factor-1 (IGF-1) and Its Monitoring in Medical Diagnostic and in Sports, Biomolecules (MDPI), https://www.mdpi.com/2218-273X/11/2/217 | Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) and long chain IGF (LR3IGF-1): Medical information and safety overview, Dopinglinkki (FINCIS Anti-Doping Authority), https://dopinglinkki.fi/en/info-bank/doping-substances/insulin-like-growth-factor-1-igf-1-and-long-chain-igf-ir3igf-1/ | Mecasermin (Subcutaneous route): Proper Use and Handling - guidance on IGF-1 administration timing with meals, Mayo Clinic / IBM Micromedex, https://www.mayoclinic.org/drugs-supplements/mecasermin-subcutaneous-route/proper-use/drg-20068117 | Detection of LongR3-IGF-I, Des(1-3)-IGF-I, and R3-IGF-I using immunopurification and high resolution mass spectrometry for antidoping purposes, Drug Testing and Analysis (PubMed), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33587816/

doses.label: 1mg
doses.dosage_text: 20-50 mcg once daily subcutaneously with gradual titration. At ~0.333 mg/mL, 20 mcg = 6 units, 50 mcg = 15 units. An 8-week protocol starts at 20 mcg/day for weeks 1-2, rises to 40 mcg/day for weeks 3-4, then holds at 50 mcg/day for weeks 5-8 -- considered the conservative upper end for most research use. Should be taken with food due to insulin-like blood sugar effects.

name: Kisspeptin
overview: Kisspeptin (also known as metastin) is a naturally occurring neuroendocrine peptide central to human reproduction, acting as the upstream trigger that stimulates GnRH secretion. It is studied in research protocols as a once-daily subcutaneous approach to support the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis.
mechanism: Kisspeptin binds GPR54 (kisspeptin) receptors on GnRH neurons in the hypothalamus, causing an immediate release of GnRH, which in turn stimulates the pituitary to secrete LH and FSH, driving sex steroid production and gametogenesis. Its action is strictly GnRH-dependent -- if GnRH release is blocked, kisspeptin cannot induce LH/FSH secretion -- meaning it works by unlocking the body's own GnRH stores rather than replacing GnRH directly.
effects: Increases endogenous testosterone and estrogen by amplifying the body's own LH/FSH signals without suppressing the HPG axis. Shows promise for restoring GnRH/LH pulsatility and resuming cycles in functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, and is used clinically to trigger a robust LH surge for oocyte maturation in IVF, potentially with lower OHSS risk than traditional hCG triggers. Generally well tolerated, with occasional mild injection-site redness or itching.
results_timeline: Not specified in source as a discrete timeline; protocols run 8-12 weeks with an initial 1-2 week lower-dose period to assess sensitivity before titrating up.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection at any consistent time, with systematic site rotation. Cycle length is kept to 8-12 weeks, avoiding prolonged continuous use to reduce the risk of tachyphylaxis.
research_notes: Emerging Therapeutic Potential of Kisspeptin and Neurokinin B, Endocrine Reviews (Oxford Academic), https://academic.oup.com/edrv/article/45/1/30/7226658 | Kisspeptin-54 triggers egg maturation in IVF with reduced OHSS risk, Journal of Clinical Investigation, https://www.jci.org/articles/view/75730 | Kisspeptin-10 Clinical Reference Sheet -- starting dose recommendations and titration guidance, https://genoracle.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/26/2024/03/Kisspeptin-10-Doctor-Sheet.pdf

doses.label: 10mg
doses.dosage_text: 100-200 mcg once daily subcutaneously. At ~3.33 mg/mL, 100 mcg = 3 units, 200 mcg = 6 units. Titration starts at 100 mcg/day for weeks 1-2 to assess sensitivity, then increases to 200 mcg/day from week 3 through week 8-12 if tolerated.

name: KPV
overview: KPV (Lysine-Proline-Valine) is the C-terminal tripeptide fragment of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH), studied for potent anti-inflammatory activity without the melanotropic (skin-pigmentation) effects of the parent hormone.
mechanism: KPV retains the anti-inflammatory portion of the alpha-MSH molecule while dropping its melanotropic activity. Preclinical work shows it reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6, IL-1beta) and modulates immune cell activity, largely through inhibition of NF-kappaB signaling and modulation of inflammatory mediator release.
effects: Reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines and modulates immune responses in preclinical models of inflammatory bowel disease and systemic inflammation. Both oral and subcutaneous routes show activity, with subcutaneous favored for systemic delivery. Some preclinical evidence suggests support for wound healing via reduced local inflammation. Generally well tolerated, with occasional mild injection-site redness or swelling and no melanotropic side effects.
results_timeline: Not specified in source with a discrete week-by-week outcomes timeline; standard protocols run 8-12 weeks with a ~4 week dose titration built in.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection at any consistent time, with systematic site rotation.
research_notes: Pawar K. et al., KPV as an alpha-MSH fragment retains potent anti-inflammatory activity without melanotropic side effects, Journal of Pharmaceutical Drug Delivery Research (2022), https://www.scitechnol.com/peer-review/recent-advances-in-kpv-peptidedelivery-8OYD.php?article_id=18216 | Brzoska T. et al., alpha-MSH and related tripeptides: modulation of colitis, inflammation, and melanocortin receptors, FASEB Journal (2003) | Dalmasso G. et al., PepT1-mediated tripeptide KPV uptake reduces intestinal inflammation in DSS colitis models, Gastroenterology (2008), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18061177/

doses.label: 10mg
doses.dosage_text: 200-500 mcg once daily subcutaneously with gradual weekly titration. At ~3.33 mg/mL, 200 mcg = 6 units, 500 mcg = 15 units. Typical schedule: 200 mcg in week 1, rising ~100 mcg per week to reach 500 mcg by weeks 4-8, which is held as the maintenance dose through the end of an 8-12 week cycle.

name: L-Carnitine
overview: L-Carnitine is an amino acid derivative essential for shuttling fatty acids into mitochondria for energy production. Subcutaneous dosing is used in research protocols to achieve near-complete bioavailability and avoid the gut-bacteria-driven TMAO production associated with oral high-dose carnitine.
mechanism: L-Carnitine acts as an obligate cofactor for the carnitine palmitoyltransferase (CPT) enzyme system, which transports long-chain fatty acids across the inner mitochondrial membrane for beta-oxidation -- a core step in converting fat stores to usable energy. Oral L-Carnitine has poor bioavailability (5-18% at high doses) and unabsorbed carnitine is converted by gut bacteria into trimethylamine, which becomes TMAO in the liver, a metabolite linked to cardiovascular risk. Subcutaneous or IV administration bypasses this pathway entirely, providing close to 100% bioavailability without TMAO production.
effects: Facilitates mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation and ATP production from fat stores. A meta-analysis of 37 randomized trials found oral dosing around 2 g/day produces modest weight loss (about 1.2 kg on average) with diminishing returns above that dose. May improve exercise capacity and reduce fatigue, particularly in carnitine-deficient populations -- clinical trials in dialysis patients using IV dosing showed increased plasma carnitine, reduced fatigue, and preserved exercise capacity over 24 weeks. Side effects are generally mild injection-site reactions; high oral doses can cause GI upset and fishy body odor, which subcutaneous dosing avoids.
results_timeline: Not specified in source as a discrete short-term timeline; benefits in the cited dialysis-patient trial were measured over a 24-week course of treatment.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, any consistent time (morning or pre-exercise commonly used). Doses above about 1.0 mL may be split into two injections at different sites for comfort. Letting the solution reach room temperature before injecting reduces discomfort.
research_notes: Sawicka AK et al., The bright and the dark sides of L-carnitine supplementation: a systematic review, Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition (2020), https://jissn.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12970-020-00377-2 | Talenezhad N et al., Meta-analysis of 37 RCTs: L-carnitine supplementation yields ~1.2 kg weight loss at ~2,000 mg/day oral dosing, Clinical Nutrition ESPEN (2020), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32359762/ | Evans AM & Fornasini G., Pharmacokinetics of L-carnitine: oral bioavailability only 5-18% at high doses vs. much higher absorption from IV/parenteral routes, Clinical Pharmacokinetics (2003), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12908852/ | Zhao Y et al., Subcutaneous L-carnitine bypasses gut bacterial TMAO production and does not promote atherosclerosis in ApoE-/- mice, unlike oral dosing, Molecular Nutrition & Food Research (2018), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29178259/

doses.label: 200mg
doses.dosage_text: 50-100 mg once daily subcutaneously, with advanced protocols using up to 200 mg daily. At 100 mg/mL, the ratio is 1 unit = 1 mg, so 50 mg = 50 units and the 100 mg maintenance dose = exactly 100 units (1.0 mL). Titration starts at 50 mg/day for weeks 1-2, then moves to the 100 mg/day maintenance dose from week 3 onward.

name: Livagen
overview: Livagen (Lys-Glu-Asp-Ala) is a synthetic tetrapeptide bioregulator studied in preclinical research for effects on peptidase inhibition and epigenetic modulation in aging tissue. No standardized human dosing protocol exists; research-context dosing is extrapolated from similar peptide bioregulators.
mechanism: Livagen strongly inhibits enkephalin-degrading enzymes in human serum, which may prolong endogenous enkephalin activity, notably without directly binding opioid receptors. In aging cells it has been shown to induce heterochromatin decondensation and reactivate ribosomal genes, and in rat liver organ cultures it enhanced cellular structure and regenerative processes.
effects: Preclinical evidence points to inhibition of enkephalin-degrading enzymes, modulation of gene expression/chromatin structure in aging tissue, and support for cellular homeostasis and regenerative capacity. In aged rodents, oral dosing normalized digestive enzyme activity toward more youthful levels. No human clinical administration studies have been reported, so the human side-effect profile is unknown; occasional mild injection-site irritation is possible with subcutaneous use.
results_timeline: Not specified in source; no human trial data exists to establish a results timeline.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection at any consistent time, with systematic site rotation.
research_notes: Effects of Livagen and Epitalon on Enkephalin-Degrading Enzymes from Human Serum, Biology Bulletin (Springer), https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1024809822681 | Effect of peptide Livagen on activity of digestive enzymes in gastrointestinal tract and non-digestive organs in rats of different ages, PubMed, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16075683/ | Functional morphology of an organotypic liver culture exposed to the peptide Livagen, PubMed, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12577697/ | Peptides and Ageing: heterochromatin decondensation and gene reactivation, ResearchGate / Neuroendocrinology Letters, https://www.researchgate.net/publication/11084943_Peptides_and_Ageing

doses.label: 20mg
doses.dosage_text: 0.5-2.0 mg once daily subcutaneously with gradual titration over 8-12 weeks. At ~6.67 mg/mL, 0.5 mg = 7.5 units, 2.0 mg = 30 units. Schedule starts at 0.5 mg/day for weeks 1-2, doubling roughly every 2 weeks (1.0 mg weeks 3-4, 1.5 mg weeks 5-6) up to a 2.0 mg/day ceiling held through week 12.

name: LL-37
overview: LL-37 (also called CAP-18) is a 37-amino-acid cationic peptide and the only cathelicidin produced naturally in humans, cleaved from the hCAP18 precursor. It is studied for broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity and immune-modulating effects, with particular research interest in wound healing and infection control.
mechanism: LL-37 is amphipathic and carries a strong net positive charge, allowing it to bind and disrupt negatively charged microbial cell membranes directly. Separately, it can bind bacterial LPS (endotoxin) and block its interaction with CD14/TLR4, dampening the inflammatory cytokine cascade that follows infection. In animal sepsis models it also triggered neutrophils to release antimicrobial-protein-rich microvesicles, which lowered bacterial burden and improved survival.
effects: Broad-spectrum antimicrobial action, modulation of inflammatory signaling from bacterial endotoxin, and support for wound-healing processes. Human trial data specifically point to faster healing in venous leg ulcers and improved granulation tissue in diabetic foot ulcers when applied topically.
results_timeline: Effects are dose- and goal-dependent; wound-healing changes in trials were assessed over multi-week treatment courses. For general immune/tissue-repair use, most protocols run 8-12 weeks before evaluating results, with the option to extend to 16 weeks.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, any consistent time of day; some protocols use a 5-days-on/2-days-off schedule instead of daily dosing.
research_notes: Kanji S et al., Journal of Immunology (PMC3836506) — Little peptide, big effects: the role of LL-37 in inflammation and autoimmune disease, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3836506/ | Innate Immunity (PMC7556193) — Antimicrobial peptide LL-37 ameliorates a murine sepsis model via microvesicle release from neutrophils, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7556193/ | Wound Repair and Regeneration (PMC9298190) — Evaluation of LL-37 in healing of hard-to-heal venous leg ulcers: a multicentric prospective RCT, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9298190/ | Archives of Dermatological Research (PMC10514151) — Efficacy of LL-37 cream in enhancing healing of diabetic foot ulcer: a randomized double-blind controlled trial, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10514151/

doses.label: 5mg
doses.dosage_text: Start around 50-100 mcg (3-6 units) once daily and increase roughly 50 mcg per week as tolerated, targeting 200-400 mcg daily by weeks 4-8. Typical cycles run 8-12 weeks, extendable to 16. At 1.67 mg/mL: 1 unit = 16.7 mcg.

name: Mazdutide
overview: Mazdutide (IBI362) is a long-acting dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist developed for chronic weight management and type 2 diabetes. It has shown 6-14% body weight reduction over 24-48 weeks in clinical trials, with mostly mild gastrointestinal side effects, and received initial regulatory approval for obesity in China.
mechanism: Mazdutide activates both GLP-1 and glucagon receptors simultaneously, combining GLP-1's appetite suppression and glucose-lowering action with the added energy-expenditure boost from glucagon receptor activity. This dual-agonist approach differentiates it from single-target GLP-1 drugs and appears to drive its strong weight-loss efficacy in trials.
effects: Significant body weight reduction (6-14% over 24-48 weeks), meaningful HbA1c improvement (~1.7%) in type 2 diabetes populations, and favorable changes in lipid profile and glucose control. Once-weekly dosing supports better adherence than daily regimens.
results_timeline: Phase 2 data showed 6.7-11.3% weight loss over 24 weeks at 3-6 mg weekly. A 48-week phase 3 trial reported 11-14% sustained weight loss at 4-6 mg weekly maintenance dosing.
timing: Once-weekly subcutaneous injection, same day each week. Volumes over 3 mL should be split across two injection sites.
research_notes: EClinicalMedicine (PubMed 36247927) — Phase 1b trial: Safety and efficacy of mazdutide 9-10 mg in Chinese adults with overweight/obesity, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36247927/ | Nature Communications — Phase 2 randomized controlled trial of mazdutide in Chinese overweight/obese adults, https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-44067-4 | New England Journal of Medicine (PubMed 40421736) — Phase 3 trial: Once-weekly mazdutide in Chinese adults with obesity or overweight (48-week results), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40421736/ | Diabetes Care (PubMed 37482087) — Phase 2 trial: Efficacy and safety of mazdutide in Chinese patients with type 2 diabetes (20-week results), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37482087/

doses.label: 5mg
doses.dosage_text: Start at 2.5 mg once weekly for the first 4 weeks, then increase to 5 mg weekly for week 5 onward as the maintenance dose. Because weekly volumes exceed a standard 100-unit pen, use a 1-3 mL syringe; split doses over 3 mL into two injection sites. At 1.67 mg/mL: 2.5 mg = 1.5 mL (150 units), 5 mg = 3.0 mL (300 units).

name: MGF
overview: Mechano Growth Factor (MGF) is a splice variant of IGF-1 (IGF-1Ec) produced locally in muscle tissue in response to mechanical stress or injury. It is studied in preclinical models for its role in muscle repair, satellite cell activation, and bone regeneration, though no completed human clinical trials exist.
mechanism: MGF arises from alternative splicing of the IGF-1 gene after mechanical loading or tissue damage, producing a peptide with a unique C-terminal E domain not found in systemic IGF-1. This E domain activates satellite cells (muscle stem cells), promotes osteoblast proliferation, and has been linked to neurogenesis in preclinical models, suggesting MGF functions as a localized, transient tissue-repair signal rather than a systemic growth factor.
effects: Preclinical research shows activation of satellite cells and myoblast proliferation in muscle tissue, support for bone-defect healing via osteoblast activity, and early evidence of neurogenesis/neuroprotection in aging brain tissue. All data is from animal or cell-culture studies.
results_timeline: No human trial data exists to anchor a timeline. Preclinical protocols generally run 8-12 weeks with gradual titration, extendable to 16 weeks; any training-related recovery changes would track with resistance-training cycles.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, any consistent time; pairing administration with resistance training is commonly discussed since mechanical loading is what naturally triggers MGF expression.
research_notes: PLOS ONE (2013) — Insulin-like growth factor I (IGF-1) Ec/Mechano Growth Factor: splice variant characterization within the growth plate, https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0076133 | International Orthopaedics (2011) — Mechano growth factor E peptide promotes osteoblast proliferation and bone-defect healing in rabbits, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00264-010-1141-2 | Molecular Brain (2017) — Mechano growth factor promotes neurogenesis in the aging mouse brain, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13041-017-0304-0 | Experimental Cell Research (2008) — MGF E-peptide activates human muscle satellite cells, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17950277/

doses.label: 5mg
doses.dosage_text: Start at 100 mcg (6 units) daily and step up roughly 50 mcg per week, reaching 250-300 mcg (15-18 units) daily by weeks 4-8. Typical cycles run 8-12 weeks, up to 16. At 1.67 mg/mL: 100 mcg = 6 units, 300 mcg = 18 units.

name: Ovagen
overview: Ovagen is a synthetic ultrashort tripeptide (Glu-Asp-Leu, or EDL) studied as a bioregulator of gastrointestinal and liver tissue. In vitro research shows it increases cell proliferation and shifts aging-related gene markers in kidney cell cultures, though no controlled human trials exist.
mechanism: As an ultrashort peptide, EDL is thought to enter cells and interact directly with DNA, modulating gene expression and protein synthesis rather than acting through a conventional surface receptor. In vitro work shows it alters aging-related gene markers (p16, p21, p53, SIRT-6) in renal cell cultures, pointing to cytoprotective effects; it has also been separately identified as an HIV-1 protease inhibitor with measurable binding affinity in the enzyme's active site.
effects: Potential support for gastrointestinal and hepatic tissue bioregulation, and in vitro evidence of cytoprotective effects through modulation of aging-related gene expression. Ultrashort peptides in this class are generally rapidly metabolized and low in immunogenicity.
results_timeline: No human trial data exists. The studied protocol uses a slow 16-week titration, with the low-dose phase in weeks 1-8 and a maintenance phase in weeks 9-16 where cumulative bioregulatory effects would theoretically be most relevant based on the underlying cell research.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, any consistent time; doses are very small (10-150 mcg) and titrated slowly, adding 10-20 mcg every 1-2 weeks as tolerated.
research_notes: MDPI International Journal of Molecular Sciences — Transport of biologically active ultrashort peptides using POT and LAT carriers, https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/23/14/7733 | PubMed — Advances in Gerontology — Tripeptides slow down aging process in renal cell culture (Khavinson et al., 2014), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25946838/ | Nature — Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy — Therapeutic peptides: current applications and future directions (Li et al., 2022), https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-00904-4 | PubMed — Biochemistry — HIV-1 protease inhibitors Glu-Asp-Leu and Glu-Asp-Phe: thermodynamic studies (Velazquez-Campoy & Freire, 2000), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11062223/

doses.label: 20mg
doses.dosage_text: Start at 10 mcg (0.1 unit) daily for weeks 1-2, then step up every 1-2 weeks to 20 mcg, 50 mcg, and 100 mcg by weeks 7-8, settling into a 100-150 mcg maintenance range for weeks 9-16. Because every dose is under 1.5 units at 10 mg/mL, a 30- or 50-unit syringe is far more readable than a standard 100-unit pen.

name: Oxytocin
overview: Oxytocin is a nonapeptide hormone best known for its roles in childbirth, lactation, and social bonding. In research settings it is studied for effects on stress, social cognition, appetite/metabolism, and pain modulation, administered here via subcutaneous injection.
mechanism: Oxytocin binds oxytocin receptors (OXTR), G-protein coupled receptors expressed centrally and peripherally. Centrally it acts as a neuromodulator influencing dopamine signaling and dampening the HPA stress axis, underlying its effects on trust and social bonding. Peripherally it contracts smooth muscle (uterine contraction, milk ejection) and appears to modulate pain and inflammation via sensory nerves or immune cells in skin tissue. It does not cross the blood-brain barrier well from the bloodstream, so subcutaneous dosing primarily engages peripheral receptors, while intranasal dosing is used in research to better reach central receptors.
effects: Increased trust and reduced social anxiety in research contexts; anxiolytic and mood-modulating properties in short-term studies; reduced caloric intake and increased fat oxidation after a single dose in human research, with animal data suggesting modest weight loss and improved insulin sensitivity with chronic use; localized analgesic effects shown in a randomized pain trial.
results_timeline: Oxytocin has rapid clearance, so most documented effects are acute and per-dose rather than cumulative. For metabolic or mood-related research goals, protocols typically run 8-12 weeks (extendable to 16) before assessing trends.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, any consistent time; effects are largely acute given oxytocin's rapid clearance rather than building up gradually.
research_notes: PubMed (38815173) — Intranasal Oxytocin for Obesity (8-week trial using 96 IU per day in adults), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38815173/ | PubMed (38642595) — Subcutaneous Oxytocin Injection Reduces Heat Pain: A Randomized-Controlled Trial, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38642595/ | PubMed (21429671) — A review of safety, side-effects and subjective reactions to intranasal oxytocin in human research, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21429671/ | PubMed (28960210) — The effects of oxytocin on eating behaviour and metabolism in humans, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28960210/

doses.label: 5mg
doses.dosage_text: Start at 100 mcg (6 units) daily for 2 weeks, then increase by about 100 mcg every 2 weeks up to a typical research ceiling of 500 mcg (30 units) daily by weeks 9-12. Cycles typically run 8-12 weeks, extendable to 16. At 1.67 mg/mL: 100 mcg = 6 units, 500 mcg = 30 units.

name: PE-22-28
overview: PE-22-28 is a synthetic heptapeptide (GVSWGLR) derived from the sortilin propeptide, engineered as a shortened, more potent analog of spadin that selectively blocks TREK-1 potassium channels. Preclinical research shows rapid antidepressant-like effects, enhanced neurogenesis, and neuroprotection with a clean cardiac and metabolic safety profile, though no human trials exist.
mechanism: PE-22-28 selectively antagonizes TREK-1 (KCNK2) two-pore-domain potassium channels, which depolarizes neurons and increases serotonergic neuron firing and monoamine transmission. In rodent studies this produces measurable increases in hippocampal neurogenesis and synaptogenesis markers within 4 days, alongside activation of CaMKII/CREB pathways linked to neuronal survival and plasticity. It is roughly 300 times more potent than its parent compound spadin (IC50 ~0.12 nM vs 40-60 nM) and has a longer duration of action (~23 hours vs ~7 hours).
effects: Rapid antidepressant-like behavioral improvements in rodent models within days, increased hippocampal neurogenesis and dendritic spine density, and neuroprotective effects in stroke models including preservation of dopaminergic neurons and prevention of post-stroke depressive behavior. Preclinical safety data show no effect on cardiac hERG channels, heart rate, blood pressure, glucose regulation, or pain perception.
results_timeline: In rodent studies, neurogenesis and synaptogenesis marker changes appear within 4 days. No human timeline exists; suggested human protocols use a 2-week low-dose start, 100 mcg maintenance through week 8, with optional escalation to 150-200 mcg through week 16.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, consistent timing, with systematic site rotation.
research_notes: Mazella J et al., PLoS Biology (2010) — Spadin, a sortilin-derived peptide targeting TREK-1 channels; novel antidepressant mechanism, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20405001/ | Moha Ou Maati H et al., Neuropharmacology (2012) — Spadin as antidepressant; absence of TREK-1-related side effects, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21807005/ | Djillani A et al., Frontiers in Pharmacology (2017) — Shortened spadin analogs (PE-22-28); superior TREK-1 inhibition, in vivo stability, antidepressant activity, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28955242/ | Pietri M et al., Neuropharmacology (2019) — Protective effects on stroke recovery and post-stroke depression induced by sortilin-derived peptides, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31325429/

doses.label: 10mg
doses.dosage_text: Start at 50 mcg (1.5 units) daily for 2 weeks to assess tolerance, then move to a 100 mcg (3 units) maintenance dose for weeks 3-8, which many researchers find sufficient given the peptide's high potency. Optional escalation to 150 mcg or 200 mcg can follow in weeks 9-16 if well tolerated. At 3.33 mg/mL: 50 mcg = 1.5 units, 200 mcg = 6 units.

name: PEG MGF
overview: PEG-MGF is a pegylated variant of Mechano Growth Factor, a muscle-derived IGF-1 splice isoform upregulated after mechanical stress or injury. Pegylation extends its half-life to allow once-daily systemic subcutaneous dosing, unlike native MGF which requires frequent local injection. All efficacy data comes from animal and cell-based studies, with no completed human trials.
mechanism: MGF's unique C-terminal E peptide activates muscle satellite cells and drives local protein synthesis to initiate tissue repair, working alongside conventional IGF-1 receptor signaling. Pegylation slows clearance of this normally short-lived, locally-acting signal, letting a single daily subcutaneous injection achieve systemic exposure instead of relying on repeated intramuscular dosing.
effects: Animal studies show enhanced muscle, bone, and cartilage regeneration with MGF analogs administered over multiple weeks, along with improved myogenic precursor cell proliferation and better muscle repair outcomes in models of induced muscle depletion or injury.
results_timeline: No human data exists to define a timeline. Preclinical protocols run a standard 8-week titration cycle, extendable to 12-16 weeks, with regeneration markers building progressively across that window in animal models.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, any consistent time; often discussed alongside resistance training since mechanical loading is what naturally triggers MGF expression.
research_notes: The Physiological Society — MGF: a local growth factor or a local tissue repair factor?, https://www.physoc.org/magazine-articles/mgf-a-local-growth-factor-or-a-local-tissue-repair-factor/ | In Vivo, PubMed (19567392) — Expression of IGF-1 isoforms after exercise-induced muscle damage in humans: characterization of the MGF E peptide actions in vitro, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19567392/ | American Journal of Transplantation, PubMed (17845560) — A synthetic mechano growth factor E peptide enhances myogenic precursor cell transplantation success, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17845560/ | Frontiers in Physiology, PubMed (31164836) — Impaired skeletal muscle regeneration induced by macrophage depletion could be partly ameliorated by MGF injection, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31164836/

doses.label: 2mg
doses.dosage_text: Start at 200 mcg (30 units) daily and increase roughly 100 mcg every 2 weeks, reaching 400-500 mcg (60-75 units) daily by weeks 5-8. Standard cycle is 8 weeks, optionally extended to 12-16. At 0.667 mg/mL doses run large in units, so a standard 1 mL insulin syringe is needed rather than a low-volume 30/50-unit pen.

name: Pinealon
overview: Pinealon is a synthetic tripeptide bioregulator (Glu-Asp-Arg) developed as part of Professor Vladimir Khavinson's peptide bioregulator research program. It is studied for neuroprotective effects, cognitive support, and gene expression modulation in brain tissue, run in short 10-20 day cycles rather than long-term weekly protocols.
mechanism: As an ultrashort tripeptide, Pinealon is believed to cross cellular and nuclear membranes and interact directly with DNA, regulating gene expression in brain tissue. Research indicates it reduces reactive oxygen species accumulation and necrotic cell death in neurons, modulates the cell cycle toward proliferative pathways, and reduces caspase-3 activity (an apoptosis-related enzyme), which may lower unnecessary neuronal cell death.
effects: Neuroprotection against oxidative stress and preservation of neuronal viability, improvements in memory and learning capacity in animal studies, and reported improvements in memory, attention, cognitive function, and emotional stability in human trials involving traumatic brain injury and cerebral disorder patients. Also linked to circadian rhythm and pineal gland function as a secondary research thread.
results_timeline: Effects are reported to typically become noticeable after 10-14 days of consistent daily administration, aligning with the mid-to-late portion of the standard 10-20 day cycle.
timing: Once-daily subcutaneous injection, preferably morning or early afternoon to align with reported cognitive benefit timing. Run in short 10-20 day cycles (extendable to 28 days), repeated every 2-3 months rather than continuously.
research_notes: Khavinson VK, Biogerontology (PubMed 19830585) — Peptide bioregulation of aging: 35-year research overview by Khavinson and Anisimov, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19830585/ | PMC (PMC3342713) — Pinealon protects rat offspring from prenatal hyperhomocysteinemia and cognitive deficits, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3342713/ | Doklady Biological Sciences (PubMed 18336762) — Regulatory peptides protect brain neurons from hypoxia in vivo, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18336762/ | PubMed (26390612) — Effects of synthetic peptides in patients with chronic polymorbidity and organic brain syndrome, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26390612/

doses.label: 20mg
doses.dosage_text: Start at 1.0 mg (15 units) daily for days 1-5, increase to 1.5 mg (22.5 units) for days 6-14, then reach 2.0 mg (30 units) for days 15-20. This is a short bioregulator cycle (10-20 days, extendable to 28), not a long weekly protocol; repeat every 2-3 months rather than continuously. At 6.67 mg/mL: 1 mg = 15 units, 2 mg = 30 units.

name: PNC-27
overview: PNC-27 is a synthetic 32-amino-acid chimeric peptide combining a p53-derived HDM-2-binding domain with a membrane-penetrating sequence, studied preclinically for selective cytotoxicity toward cancer cells expressing abnormal p53/HDM-2 complexes. No human clinical trials exist, and the FDA has explicitly warned that PNC-27 products are unapproved with no established safety data.
mechanism: PNC-27 fuses a segment of the p53 tumor suppressor protein (residues 12-26) to a membrane-penetrating leader sequence. Preclinical studies indicate it selectively binds HDM-2 expressed on the surface of cancer cells, causing membrane destabilization and necrotic cell death via direct membrane lysis rather than the conventional apoptosis pathway, while reportedly sparing normal cells.
effects: Preclinical studies suggest selective cytotoxicity toward cancer cells expressing abnormal p53/HDM-2, with mouse tumor model research showing inhibited cancer cell growth via intraperitoneal administration. No human efficacy or safety data exists in any form.
results_timeline: Not specified in source. No human trials have been conducted, so no legitimate timeline for human effects exists; all timing data below is a speculative extrapolation, not a validated finding.
timing: Not specified in source beyond a proposed once-daily subcutaneous schedule; the source explicitly states any human dosing above a few hundred micrograms per day is speculative, and PNC-27 has no established human safety profile.
administration: Subcutaneous (per proposed educational protocol; underlying animal research used intraperitoneal administration)
research_notes: Journal of Biomedical Science (2017) — Review of therapeutic peptides; PNC-27 mechanism and cancer cell selectivity, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12929-017-0328-x | Cancer Chemotherapy and Pharmacology (2010) — PNC-27 induces tumor cell membrane lysis; preclinical mechanism study, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20182728/ | FDA Drug Safety Communication (2015) — Warning: PNC-27 is unapproved; safety not established, https://www.fda.gov/drugs/drug-safety-and-availability/fda-warns-cancer-patients-not-use-pnc-27-products-treatment | BMC Cancer (2008) — PNC-27 selectively kills cancer cells via HDM-2 binding and membrane disruption, https://bmccancer.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2407-8-237

doses.label: 30mg
doses.dosage_text: No validated human dosing exists. A speculative educational schedule extrapolated from animal data starts at 100 mcg (1 unit) daily and rises by roughly 100 mcg every 2-4 weeks toward 500 mcg (5 units) by weeks 13-16. The source is explicit that this is theoretical, not an established protocol, and that the FDA warns against use of PNC-27 products entirely. At 10 mg/mL: 100 mcg = 1 unit, 500 mcg = 5 units.

name: Prostamax
overview: Prostamax is a synthetic tetrapeptide (Lys-Glu-Asp-Pro, or KEDP) belonging to the Khavinson-style class of bioregulator peptides, originally derived from research into prostate tissue peptide complexes. It has been studied in animal models for supporting prostate tissue health, particularly reducing inflammation associated with prostatitis and BPH.
mechanism: Prostamax is theorized to act through epigenetic mechanisms, interacting with chromatin structure and histone proteins to modulate gene expression rather than working through a conventional receptor-ligand pathway. In preclinical rat models, intramuscular KEDP administration reduced inflammatory markers and appeared to help prevent fibrotic changes in prostate tissue.
effects: In animal prostatitis models, reduced inflammation and supported normalization of prostate tissue. In BPH models, favorable effects were observed on prostate weight and histological parameters. No human clinical trial data exist, so effects in people are extrapolated from these animal studies rather than confirmed.
results_timeline: Not specified in source (based on preclinical studies running 15-60 days of daily administration in animal models; no human timeline data available).
timing: Administered once daily at a consistent time, intramuscularly into large muscle groups (rotate between deltoid, vastus lateralis, glute).
administration: Intramuscular (subcutaneous mentioned as an alternative route)
research_notes: Zhurbenko et al., Modern Research in Inflammation (2013) — experimental study of Prostamax in chronic aseptic prostatitis therapy, https://doi.org/10.4236/mri.2013.23007 | Modern Research in Inflammation (2014) — experimental study of KEDP tetrapeptide in a benign prostatic hyperplasia model, https://doi.org/10.4236/mri.2014.33013 | Bulletin of Experimental Biology and Medicine (2004) — effects of short peptides on lymphocyte chromatin in senile subjects, https://doi.org/10.1023/B:BEBM.0000024393.40560.05 | Stem Cell Reviews and Reports (2020) — peptide regulation of cell differentiation and epigenetic mechanisms of short bioregulatory peptides, https://doi.org/10.1007/s12015-019-09938-8

doses.label: 20mg
doses.dosage_text: 500 mcg to 1 mg once daily via intramuscular injection, titrated up gradually over 8-12 weeks (extendable to 16). At 10 mg/mL: start at 5 units (500 mcg) for weeks 1-2, step up to 7.5 units (750 mcg) for weeks 3-4, then reach 10 units (1 mg) by weeks 5-8 as a maintenance dose. Use a 30- or 50-unit syringe for better precision at these low volumes.

name: SLU-PP-332
overview: SLU-PP-332 is a synthetic small-molecule pan-agonist of the estrogen-related receptors (ERRalpha, ERRbeta, and ERRgamma), studied exclusively in mouse models for its ability to trigger an exercise-mimetic gene program that boosts mitochondrial function, endurance, and cardiometabolic health. As of the source material, no human trials have been conducted, so all information here reflects preclinical animal research only.
mechanism: The compound acts as a pan-agonist across all three ERR subtypes, with the strongest activity at ERRalpha, activating the transcriptional program normally triggered by aerobic exercise. In mouse studies this increased mitochondrial respiration and fatty-acid oxidation in skeletal muscle without requiring physical exertion.
effects: In murine studies: improved endurance and treadmill running capacity, enhanced mitochondrial function and fatty-acid oxidation, improved glucose tolerance in diet-induced obese mice, improved cardiac fatty-acid metabolism and mitochondrial function in heart failure models, and reversal of mitochondrial dysfunction and inflammation in aging kidney models. No human safety or efficacy data exist.
results_timeline: Not specified in source — all timeline data comes from acute-to-8-week murine study designs, not human use.
timing: Not applicable to humans; published murine protocols used twice-daily intraperitoneal dosing at consistent intervals.
administration: Intraperitoneal in published animal studies; subcutaneous route is not validated for this compound in humans
research_notes: Billon et al., ACS Chemical Biology (2023) — synthetic ERRa/b/g agonist induces an ERRalpha-dependent acute aerobic exercise response and enhances exercise capacity, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36988910/ | Journal of Pharmacology and Experimental Therapeutics (2024) — a synthetic ERR agonist alleviates metabolic syndrome, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37739806/ | Circulation (2024) — novel pan-ERR agonists ameliorate heart failure through enhancing cardiac fatty acid metabolism and mitochondrial function, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37961903/ | American Journal of Pathology (2023) — estrogen-related receptor agonism reverses mitochondrial dysfunction and inflammation in the aging kidney, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37717940/

doses.label: 5mg
doses.dosage_text: No validated human dosing exists. Published murine studies used 25-50 mg/kg intraperitoneally, twice daily. Scaled to a 25g mouse and this vial's 1.67 mg/mL concentration, that works out to roughly 625 mcg (37.5 units) per injection during an initial two-week phase, then 1.25 mg (75 units) per injection twice daily for weeks 3-8 in the murine-equivalent framework. This is preclinical research information only, not a human protocol.

name: SNAP-8
overview: SNAP-8 (Acetyl Octapeptide-3) is a synthetic octapeptide developed as a cosmeceutical anti-wrinkle ingredient. It is an extended, more potent analog of the hexapeptide Argireline, designed to mimic part of the SNAP-25 protein involved in neurotransmitter release at neuromuscular junctions.
mechanism: SNAP-8 competitively inhibits SNAP-25 within the SNARE complex, reducing acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junctions. This relaxes the small facial muscles responsible for expression lines without causing full paralysis the way botulinum toxin does, since it does not cleave SNAP-25 but rather competes for the same binding site. Lab work found it roughly 30% more effective at reducing neurotransmitter release than Argireline, with better formulation stability.
effects: Reduces the depth of expression lines and wrinkles while preserving natural facial movement. Topical studies reported wrinkle depth reductions averaging around 35%, with peak improvements near 60% in some areas after about a month. Generally well tolerated with only occasional mild injection-site irritation reported for the subcutaneous route.
results_timeline: Noticeable smoothing of expression lines typically appears after 2-4 weeks of consistent daily use, with continued improvement through 8-12 weeks as dosing reaches its target level.
timing: Once daily at any consistent time, subcutaneously, with gradual dose titration over 8-12 weeks (extendable to 16).
research_notes: International Journal of Cosmetic Science — Argireline hexapeptide with antiwrinkle activity in a human study, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18498523/ | Annals of Dermatology — clinical safety and efficacy of a dissolving microneedle patch containing SNAP-8 in a 24-subject trial, https://doi.org/10.5021/ad.23.136 | Journal of Drugs in Dermatology — open-label 14-week trial of a multi-peptide serum including SNAP-8, https://jddonline.com/articles/an-open-label-clinical-trial-of-a-peptide-treatment-serum-and-supporting-regimen-designed-to-improve-S1545961616P1100X | Frontiers in Chemistry — cosmeceutical peptides review covering SNAP-8's SNARE-complex mechanism and comparative potency versus Argireline, https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fchem.2020.572923/full

doses.label: 10mg
doses.dosage_text: 330 mcg to 1 mg once daily via subcutaneous injection, titrated gradually over 8-12 weeks. At 3.33 mg/mL: start at 10 units (330 mcg) for the first 4 weeks, increase to 15 units (500 mcg) for weeks 5-8, and reach 30 units (1 mg) by weeks 9-12 for maximal effect. Protocol can extend to 16 weeks at the top dose.

name: SS-31
overview: SS-31 (elamipretide) is a mitochondria-targeted tetrapeptide that binds cardiolipin in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It has shown protective effects in preclinical models of heart failure, neurodegeneration, kidney disease, and age-related muscle atrophy, and received FDA accelerated approval in 2025 (as Forzinity) as the first treatment for Barth syndrome.
mechanism: SS-31 selectively accumulates in the inner mitochondrial membrane, binding cardiolipin — a phospholipid essential for organizing electron transport chain supercomplexes and maintaining cristae structure. By stabilizing these cardiolipin-protein interactions, it improves electron transport efficiency, reduces pathological reactive oxygen species generation, and enhances ATP synthesis in metabolically demanding tissues.
effects: Stabilizes mitochondrial membranes and supports electron transport chain function, increasing ATP output and lowering oxidative stress in metabolically active tissue. In Barth syndrome patients, produced measurable gains in muscle strength and six-minute walk distance. Phase II trials in heart failure and primary mitochondrial myopathy did not meet their primary efficacy endpoints despite a favorable safety profile. Does not meaningfully change blood pressure, heart rate, or standard lab values.
results_timeline: Not specified in source for general use; clinical trials assessing functional outcomes ran 4-12 weeks, with the Barth syndrome (TAZPOWER) trial showing significant strength and walk-distance improvements over its trial duration.
timing: Once daily subcutaneous injection at a consistent time; doses above 10 mg should be split into two injections at sites at least two inches apart.
research_notes: International Journal of Molecular Sciences (MDPI) — comprehensive review of elamipretide structure, mechanism of action, and therapeutic potential, https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/26/3/944 | U.S. Food & Drug Administration — FDA grants accelerated approval to elamipretide (Forzinity) as first treatment for Barth syndrome (2025), https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-grants-accelerated-approval-first-treatment-barth-syndrome | Journal of the American College of Cardiology (JACC) — PROGRESS-HF trial: safety and tolerability of elamipretide in heart failure patients, https://www.jacc.org/doi/10.1016/j.jchf.2020.02.009 | Neurology (AAN Journals) — MMPOWER-2 trial: elamipretide in primary mitochondrial myopathy, https://n.neurology.org/content/94/7/e733

doses.label: 10mg
doses.dosage_text: 5-10 mg once daily via subcutaneous injection for standard protocols; advanced protocols under medical supervision may reach 15-20 mg/day. At 10 mg/mL: 50 units = 5 mg, 100 units = 10 mg (a full syringe). Doses above 10 mg (100 units) exceed a single U-100 syringe and must be split into two separate injections at different sites. Standard protocols run 8-12 weeks; data beyond 12 weeks is limited.

name: Survodutide
overview: Survodutide (BI 456906) is an investigational dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist under development for obesity and metabolic liver disease. In phase 2 trials, once-weekly dosing produced dose-dependent weight loss of 12.5-14.9% versus placebo over 46 weeks.
mechanism: The GLP-1 component enhances satiety and improves glycemic control, while the glucagon receptor component increases energy expenditure and promotes hepatic lipid oxidation. This dual mechanism is hypothesized to provide additive or synergistic effects on body weight and liver fat compared to GLP-1 agonism alone.
effects: Dose-dependent weight loss of 12.5-14.9% versus placebo over 46 weeks in phase 2 obesity trials, along with improvements in liver-related biomarkers in NAFLD/NASH populations. A meta-analysis confirmed consistent weight-loss efficacy across multiple randomized controlled trials. Adverse effects are primarily gastrointestinal (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation), most common during dose escalation.
results_timeline: Not specified in source beyond the 46-week phase 2 trial window showing the full weight-loss effect; gradual titration over the first 10-12 weeks is intended to reduce GI side effects along the way.
timing: Once weekly subcutaneous injection, same day each week, with rotating injection sites; gradual dose titration over 10-12 weeks to a maintenance dose.
research_notes: Le Roux et al., Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology (2024) — phase 2 trial of once-weekly subcutaneous survodutide (0.6-4.8 mg) in obesity showing dose-dependent weight loss of 12.5-14.9%, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38330987/ | Guo et al., Journal of Hepatology (2023) — efficacy, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics of survodutide in NAFLD/NASH patients, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38857788/ | Wharton et al., Obesity (Silver Spring) (2024) — SYNCHRONIZE-1 & 2 phase 3 trial design for survodutide in obesity, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39495965/ | Wan et al., Diabetology & Metabolic Syndrome (2024) — meta-analysis of survodutide weight-loss efficacy across randomized controlled trials, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13098-024-01501-x

doses.label: 10mg
doses.dosage_text: 0.6 mg to 6.0 mg once weekly via subcutaneous injection, titrated gradually over 10-12+ weeks. At 5 mg/mL: 12 units = 0.6 mg, 96 units = 4.8 mg. The 6.0 mg maintenance dose (120 units / 1.2 mL) exceeds a standard 100-unit insulin syringe and requires a 1-3 mL Luer-lock syringe or splitting into two injections.

name: Testagen
overview: Testagen is a synthetic tetrapeptide (Lys-Glu-Asp-Gly, or KEDG), an anterior pituitary-derived bioregulator studied for its role in modulating endocrine function, particularly the pituitary-gonadal axis. Preclinical research points to effects on thyroid hormone normalization and male reproductive hormone modulation.
mechanism: Testagen belongs to a class of short bioregulatory peptides hypothesized to act through gene-regulatory mechanisms influencing hormone synthesis and secretion, rather than through a conventional receptor-based pathway. Preclinical avian research showed it could normalize thyroid hormone levels in hypophysectomized (pituitary-removed) subjects and modulate signaling along the pituitary-gonadal axis.
effects: In avian preclinical models, normalization of thyroid hormone parameters in hypophysectomized subjects and apparent modulation of male reproductive hormone activity via pituitary pathways. No published human safety or efficacy data exist, so the human effect profile is unknown.
results_timeline: Not specified in source — no human trial timeline exists; animal studies do not translate directly to a human onset timeline.
timing: Once daily at a consistent time, subcutaneously, with gradual titration over 8-12 weeks (extendable to 16).
research_notes: MDPI Molecules — the inhibitory effect and adsorption properties of Testagen peptide on copper surfaces (structural characterization), https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules30153141 | MDPI Molecules — peptide regulation of gene expression: a systematic review, https://www.mdpi.com/1420-3049/26/22/7053 | PubMed — effects of hypophyseal Lys-Glu-Asp-Gly peptide on immunity, hemostasis, and thyroid function in hypophysectomized chickens, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20731122/ | PubMed — effects of Lys-Glu-Asp-Gly peptide on hormonal activity and thyroid morphology in hypophysectomized mature and old birds, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21809626/

doses.label: 20mg
doses.dosage_text: 100-300 mcg once daily via subcutaneous injection, titrated gradually over 8-12 weeks. At 6.67 mg/mL: start at 1.5 units (100 mcg) for weeks 1-2, step up to 2.25 units (150 mcg) for weeks 3-4, reach 3 units (200 mcg) by weeks 5-8, with an optional push to 3.75-4.5 units (250-300 mcg) for weeks 9-12. Given the very low unit counts, a 30- or 50-unit syringe is recommended for accuracy.

name: Thymosin Alpha-1
overview: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Ta1) is a 28-amino-acid peptide originally isolated from the thymus gland, recognized for broad immunomodulatory properties. It has been investigated as an immune enhancer in chronic viral infections (hepatitis B/C, HIV/AIDS) and critical illness including sepsis and COVID-19.
mechanism: Thymosin Alpha-1 enhances the maturation and differentiation of T-cells, augments dendritic cell function, and promotes production of key cytokines including interferon-alpha and interleukin-2, modulating immune function through multiple coordinated pathways.
effects: Supports enhanced T-cell function and overall immune competence. Clinical studies have shown it enhances immune responses in immunocompromised individuals, including those with chronic hepatitis B and C. Meta-analyses found it reduced mortality in moderate-to-critical COVID-19 patients. It has demonstrated an excellent safety profile, with doses up to 1.6 mg twice weekly for 6-12 months well tolerated, and no significant compound-specific toxicity observed even at experimental doses up to 16 mg subcutaneously over 12 months.
results_timeline: Not specified in source as a standalone timeline; clinical benefit in the studied conditions (chronic viral infection, critical illness) was evaluated over the course of multi-week to multi-month treatment courses.
timing: Once daily subcutaneous injection at a consistent time; an 8-week protocol starts at a lower dose in week 1 to gauge tolerance before increasing to a maintenance dose from week 2 onward. Treatment durations of 8-16 weeks are commonly reported.
research_notes: Dominari et al., World Journal of Virology (2020) — comprehensive review of Thymosin Alpha-1 mechanisms, dosing (0.8-6.4 mg SC), and clinical applications, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7747025/ | Tao et al., Molecules (2023) — Thymosin Alpha-1 in viral diseases: mechanisms and therapeutic applications in HBV, HCV, and HIV, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10144284/ | Soeroto et al., Inflammopharmacology (2023) — meta-analysis of Ta1 in COVID-19 showing significant mortality reduction in moderate-to-critical patients, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589223/ | Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences — Thymosin Alpha-1: biological activities, clinical applications, and pharmacokinetics review, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17804536/

doses.label: 5mg
doses.dosage_text: 300-500 mcg once daily via subcutaneous injection. At 1.67 mg/mL: start at 18 units (300 mcg) during week 1 to assess tolerance, then move to 30 units (500 mcg) daily from week 2 onward as maintenance. The 500 mcg/day dose works out to roughly 3.5 mg/week, consistent with typical clinical dosing ranges. Treatment courses commonly run 8-16 weeks; reconstituted solution should be used within 7 days.

name: Vesugen
overview: Vesugen (Lys-Glu-Asp, or KED) is a tripeptide bioregulator synthesized at the Saint Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology, derived from amino acids associated with vascular wall proteins. Research suggests it may support vascular endothelial cell renewal and has also shown neuroprotective signals in preliminary studies.
mechanism: Vesugen is thought to support vascular endothelial cell proliferation by modulating expression of Ki-67, a protein closely tied to cell division, potentially through epigenetic interaction with the Ki-67 gene's promoter region. Additional research suggests it may normalize endothelin-1 expression and increase sirtuin-1 (SIRT-1) expression in vascular endothelial cells.
effects: May support vascular endothelial cell proliferation and renewal through Ki-67 gene regulation. Preliminary research suggests neuroprotective properties — oral application improved memory and attention in elderly individuals with functional CNS disorders in one study, and it may restore synaptic plasticity in in vitro Alzheimer's disease models. Generally well tolerated, with occasional mild injection-site reactions.
results_timeline: Not specified in source for the injectable protocol; cited neuroprotective findings come from separate oral-administration research in elderly subjects rather than the dosing schedule described here.
timing: Once daily at a consistent time, subcutaneously, with gradual titration over 8-12 weeks (extendable to 16).
research_notes: Khavinson et al., Bulletin of Experimental Biology and Medicine (2016) — short peptides regulate gene expression, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27909961/ | Advances in Gerontology (2014) — epigenetic aspects of peptidergic regulation of vascular endothelial cell proliferation during aging, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25051766/ | Advances in Gerontology (2016) — molecular aspects of vasoprotective peptide KED activity during atherosclerosis and restenosis, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28539025/ | Bulletin of Experimental Biology and Medicine (2021) — peptide KED: molecular-genetic aspects of neurogenesis regulation in Alzheimer's disease, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34173097/

doses.label: 20mg
doses.dosage_text: 500 mcg to 2 mg once daily via subcutaneous injection, titrated gradually over 8-12 weeks. At 6.67 mg/mL: start at 7.5 units (500 mcg) in week 1, increase to 15 units (1 mg) in week 2, 22.5 units (1.5 mg) in week 3, and reach 22.5-30 units (1.5-2 mg) as a maintenance range from week 4 through week 8 or beyond. A 50-unit syringe with half-unit gradations helps with the smaller early doses.

name: Vilon
overview: Vilon (Lys-Glu) is a synthetic immunoregulatory dipeptide made of lysine and glutamic acid residues, one of the shortest bioregulator peptides studied by Russian gerontology researchers. Preclinical work suggests it may enhance immune markers including CD5+ lymphocytes and interleukin-2 expression in thymic and splenic cell cultures.
mechanism: Vilon is proposed to modulate lymphocyte populations and cytokine gene expression. In vitro studies show it increases CD5+ cell counts in thymus cell cultures and upregulates interleukin-2 (IL-2) mRNA expression in splenocyte preparations. Additional preclinical work suggests effects on TGF-beta1 and vascular permeability in renal stress models.
effects: Supports immune cell marker expression (CD5+ lymphocytes) and IL-2 gene activity in cell culture models. May modulate inflammatory cytokines such as TGF-beta1 under experimental renal stress conditions. Early mouse studies suggest possible tumor growth inhibition and lifespan extension, though human data remain limited. Generally well tolerated in preclinical settings, with only occasional mild injection-site reactions.
results_timeline: Not specified in source for human use; the pulsed cycle protocol itself (5 days on, roughly 23 days off, run for 2-4 cycles) is analogized from related short-peptide regimens such as Epitalon rather than derived from human timeline data.
timing: Once daily subcutaneous injection for 5 consecutive days per 4-week cycle, then a rest period for the remainder of the cycle; repeat for 2-4 cycles total.
research_notes: Sevostianova et al. (2013) — immunomodulating effects of Vilon and its analogue in the culture of human and animal thymus cells, https://doi.org/10.1007/s10517-013-2000-0 | Kazakova et al. (2002) — in vitro effect of short peptides on expression of interleukin-2 gene in splenocytes, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1020210615148 | Gavrisheva et al. (2005) — effect of Vilon peptide on TGF-beta1 and vascular permeability in chronic renal failure, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16027222/ | Chernyak et al. (2000) — synthetic dipeptide Vilon inhibits tumor growth and prolongs life in mice, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10994281/

doses.label: 20mg
doses.dosage_text: 67-667 mcg once daily via subcutaneous injection, given only on 5 consecutive days per 4-week cycle (pulsed, not continuous). At 6.67 mg/mL: cycle 1 titrates up day by day from 1 unit (67 mcg) on day 1 to 5 units (333 mcg) on day 5. From cycle 2 onward, daily dose during the 5 injection days runs 5-10 units (333-667 mcg). Repeat for 2-4 total cycles. A 30- or 50-unit syringe is recommended given the very low unit counts.
